claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let's start with the basics.

**Question 1: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility depends on the child's age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to ask about, please let me know their ages as well.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 16 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 16, he falls into the 16-19 age category, which means we need to check if he meets the education or training requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

Let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 2: Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

For 16-19 year olds to qualify for Child Benefit, they need to either:
- Be currently enrolled in approved education or training, OR
- Have recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)

So, which of these applies to Alex?
1. **He's currently in approved education or training**
2. **He recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - he's not in education/training and hasn't been recently

What's Alex's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently in education. He's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college. Let me move forward to check if this type of education qualifies for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are approved qualifications for Child Benefit. Now I need to verify a few details about Alex's education to make sure it meets all the requirements:

**Question 3: Can you confirm the following about Alex's A-level studies?**

1. **Is it full-time education?** (At least 12 hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is this education funded or arranged by an employer?**
3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?**

A-levels at sixth form college typically meet the requirements, but I need to confirm these specific details to ensure Alex qualifies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's full-time - he's there during the week like a normal school day. No, it's not funded by an employer - he's just at the local sixth form college. And no, he's not married or in a civil partnership.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Alex's A-level studies meet all the requirements:
- ✓ A-levels are approved (non-advanced) qualifications
- ✓ Full-time education (12+ hours per week of supervised study)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married/in civil partnership

Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 4: Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week (or is he not in paid work)?**

For Child Benefit eligibility, young people aged 16+ in education must work less than 24 hours per week. Working 24 hours or more per week would make them ineligible.

Does Alex:
- Work less than 24 hours per week, OR
- Not have any paid work at all?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't have any paid work at all. He's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is focused on his studies and not working, which meets this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 5: Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, is Alex receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), OR
- Universal Credit

in his own name?

If Alex is **NOT** receiving these benefits, that's good for eligibility purposes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't receiving any benefits in his own right. He's still living at home with me and I support him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Alex is not receiving benefits in his own right. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 6: Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

You mentioned he's studying A-levels at sixth form college, which sounds like he's not in an apprenticeship program. Can you confirm:

- Is Alex in an apprenticeship of any kind, OR
- Is he just doing regular A-levels (not an apprenticeship)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship. He's just doing regular A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is doing regular A-levels, not an apprenticeship. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 7: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you (the claimant) need to meet one of the following residency requirements:

**The most common is:**
- You normally live in the UK

**Or you meet one of these special circumstances:**
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain work/income requirements
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

**Do you normally live in the UK?** If yes, that would meet the residency requirement.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK (Manchester).

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 8: Are you responsible for Alex?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to be responsible for the child. This is satisfied if you meet **any** of these:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you mentioned you do - he lives at home with you)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, this meets the responsibility requirement. Can you confirm that Alex does live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the responsibility requirement as Alex lives at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 9: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

If someone else is claiming, you would need to either:
- Have them transfer the claim to you (if you both agree), OR
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question 10: What is Alex's current living situation?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex currently **in hospital**?
- Is Alex currently **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **fostered**?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you (which is what you've indicated)?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Can you confirm this is the case?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Alex is just living normally at home with me. He's not in hospital, not in care, and not fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living normally at home with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final Question 11: Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would be things like:
- The council paying for his housing
- The council contributing to his living costs or maintenance

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you and you support him, I assume the council is **NOT** paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. I'm supporting him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me now provide you with the final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

## Summary for Alex (Age 16)

**Result: ELIGIBLE ✓**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for your son Alex because:

✓ **Alex is a qualifying young person** - He's 16 and in approved full-time education (A-levels)  
✓ **You meet the claimant requirements** - You live in the UK and are responsible for Alex  
✓ **No conflicting circumstances** - No one else is claiming, and no special situations apply

---

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

**Important:** Be aware of the **High Income Child Benefit Charge** if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year. This means you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through your tax return, but you can still claim to protect your National Insurance credits.

**Payment details:** Child Benefit is typically paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

---

## How to Apply:

You can find the application form and more information here:  
**https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Congratulations, and best of luck with your claim! If you have any other questions, feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 16 years old, falling within the 16-19 age range for extended eligibility; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college); (3) His education is full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study); (4) His education is not employer-funded; (5) He is not married or in a civil partnership; (6) He is not working (works less than 24 hours per week); (7) He is not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit); (8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (9) The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester); (10) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him at home); (11) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (12) Alex is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (13) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex (age 16). Alex meets all the requirements for a qualifying young person aged 16-19 as he is in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels). The claimant meets all claimant requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no conflicting claims or council support.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 16 years old, falling within the 16-19 age range for extended eligibility; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college); (3) His education is full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study); (4) His education is not employer-funded; (5) He is not married or in a civil partnership; (6) He is not working (works less than 24 hours per week); (7) He is not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit); (8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (9) The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester); (10) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him at home); (11) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (12) Alex is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (13) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex (age 16). Alex meets all the requirements for a qualifying young person aged 16-19 as he is in approved full-time non-advanced education (A-levels). The claimant meets all claimant requirements including UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no conflicting claims or council support.
